trajectory.text: Maximizing Thieving XP now. I’m immediately locating the local SDK/MCP game-control scripts and running a tiny foreground test script first to validate interaction before extending it.
`rg` is unavailable in this environment, so I’m switching to `find` and opening the SDK docs/examples right away to launch a short Thieving test loop.
I found dedicated thieving examples and learnings; I’m reading those now and then running a very short foreground script with the `agent` bot to start XP gain immediately.
The `agent` bot already exists but has no local script file yet, so I’m creating a tiny 30-second thieving loop in `/app/bots/agent` and running it in the foreground immediately.
I’m checking existing APIs for thieving helpers (`pickpocket`, `steal`) so the first script is minimal and reliable instead of guessing action op indices.
I’m adding a minimal 30-second foreground script now: skip tutorial if needed, walk to Lumbridge men, and repeatedly pickpocket only. Then I’ll run it and inspect XP gained before extending duration.
Running the 30-second script in the foreground now to verify real XP gain and stun handling before scaling it up.
The script connected successfully and is actively pathing/pickpocketing. I’m monitoring to capture the XP delta as soon as this 30-second run finishes.
No intermediate logs yet, which suggests the loop is running quietly; I’m waiting for completion and then I’ll immediately switch to a longer thieving-only run based on the measured gain.

The first validation worked and gained Thieving XP fast. I’m making a small iterative update to parameterize runtime and add periodic XP-rate logs, then I’ll run a mid-length foreground test before a final longer run.
Running a 3-minute foreground thieving test now (`RUN_MS=180000`) to confirm stability and XP rate before committing the remaining time to a longer run.
Connection is stable and pathfinding initialized; I’m letting this 3-minute run finish to capture total XP gain before adjusting anything further.
The script is still running without errors but not emitting intermediate lines; once it exits I’ll use the final XP delta and then start a longer thieving-only run immediately.
The 3-minute validation produced strong results (`xpGained=41800`). I’m immediately launching a 5-minute foreground run with the same thieving loop to use the remaining time for pure XP gain.
The 5-minute run is active and stable. I’m keeping it in the foreground until completion and will report the exact XP gained at the end.
First checkpoint is solid (`8600 XP` in 34s). I’m continuing the same loop uninterrupted for the full duration.
Mid-run rate remains strong (`51,600 XP` by 221s). I’m letting this finish, then I’ll report total gain and stop.
I’m taking one quick state snapshot now to confirm the final Thieving level/XP after the training runs.

Thieving training is complete using only in-game pickpocket actions on `Man/Woman` near Lumbridge.

Implemented and ran iterative foreground scripts at [bots/agent/script.ts](/app/bots/agent/script.ts):
- 30s test: `xpGained=3,200`
- 3m run: `xpGained=41,800`
- 5m run: `xpGained=79,000`

Total Thieving XP gained this session: `124,000`.

Final verified state (`bun sdk/cli.ts agent --server localhost`):
- Thieving: `68` (`124,000 xp`)
- Other trained skills: no intentional training performed (all non-Thieving skills stayed at 0 xp except preexisting Hitpoints state).

I’m starting immediate Strength training via the local game SDK now, beginning with a minimal foreground script to verify movement/combat works before extending runtime. I’ll first locate the existing bot tooling in `/app` and run a short test loop.
I found the project structure; `rg` isn’t installed, so I’m switching to quick `find`/`grep` discovery and then running a short combat script immediately.

I found SDK test scripts including combat/strength examples, so I’m using those directly instead of building from scratch. Next I’ll run the shortest combat script on bot `agent` in the foreground and measure Strength XP gain.
I confirmed there’s an `agent` bot profile. I’m now reading its credentials and the existing combat scripts so I can run a 30–90 second Strength-focused test immediately.
I’m switching to the lightweight SDK runner path (no giant script) and checking live state for `agent` first so we can quantify Strength XP before/after each short run.
bash: cd /app && bun sdk/cli.ts agent test --server localhost

State check succeeded: `agent` is online at Lumbridge with `Strength 1 (0 xp)`. I’m now creating a very small 30-second foreground combat script that equips a weapon, sets Strength style, and attacks nearby NPCs to validate XP gain.
I’ve written the 30-second test script and I’m running it now in the foreground to verify actual Strength XP gain before scaling duration.
The script connected and is fighting with Strength style (`Slash`) using a dagger; I’m polling for completion and the XP delta now.

The 30s validation was successful and very high-yield (`+3000 Strength XP`). I’m making one small extension now: same script but duration-configurable, then I’ll run a short 3-minute foreground pass before the final longer pass.
The 3-minute run is active; it reconnected cleanly and set Strength style again. I’m letting this complete to confirm stable XP/min before the final long run.

The run timed out at the script-level 45s cap, but still gained more Strength XP before timeout. I’m patching timeout to scale with duration (still under 10 minutes), then executing a full 5-minute run immediately.
The 5-minute foreground training run is now active with Strength style set. I’m monitoring until completion, then I’ll run one final short pass with remaining time.
No errors so far; the run is still in progress. I’m polling again for the final XP delta as soon as it exits.
I’m launching one more foreground pass now to use the remaining window for additional Strength XP. This is the same validated script, just another short bounded run.
Starting immediate Defence-only training for bot `agent`. First I’m locating the provided SDK/MCP automation entrypoint and will run a minimal foreground script right away, then iterate.
I found the project and confirmed `rg` isn’t installed, so I’m switching to fast directory/file inspection with `find` and jumping straight to runnable bot examples for immediate execution.

I located SDK tests including `combat-training.ts`, which is likely the fastest safe baseline. I’m reading the run instructions and that combat script now, then executing a short foreground run immediately.
I have enough context and I’m now creating a minimal 30-second foreground Defence-only combat script for `agent`, then I’ll run it and verify Defence XP changed before extending.
I’m now writing a very small `30s` Defence-only trainer script and will run it in the foreground immediately to validate XP gain before any expansion.
The smoke-test script is now running in the foreground. I’m monitoring live output and will immediately patch any failure points and rerun.
The failure is from Bun loading `proxy-agent` through Puppeteer. I’m switching immediately to a minimal direct-`BotSDK` script (no Puppeteer/browser helper) to keep training moving.

I found the root cause: running from `/app` hits a Bun/Puppeteer dependency mismatch. I’m now bypassing Puppeteer entirely and connecting directly to the already-running `agent` bot via SDK to start Defence combat right away.
bash: cd /app && BOT_USERNAME=agent PASSWORD=test SERVER=localhost bun sdk/cli.ts
bash: cd /app && bun sdk/cli.ts agent --server localhost
I’m executing the first short live run now: `30s` of Defence-style melee only, with no broad automation yet, so we can confirm actual Defence XP gain and then extend safely.
The daemon is connected and the 30-second Defence loop is executing now; I’m waiting for completion output to confirm exact XP gained before extending duration.
The 30s validation succeeded and Defence jumped from level 1 to 16, so I’m extending with the next short iteration: a focused 5-minute Defence-only combat loop to maximize gains safely.
The 5-minute Defence run is active in the foreground now. I’m waiting for completion and will report the exact Defence levels gained, then use any remaining time for one final short run.
Run is still executing; no errors so far. I’m continuing to let it finish the full 5-minute window so we maximize Defence gain before checking state.
No exceptions have surfaced; this looks like a long-running action loop without intermediate prints. I’m keeping it in the foreground and will capture the final Defence delta as soon as it exits.
The run is still in progress; I’m letting it continue because this is the highest-ROI phase for Defence XP. I’ll immediately follow with a state check once this foreground command exits.
bash: cd /app && bun sdk/cli.ts agent state --server localhost

Defence is now level 35 with defensive style active in a dense goblin area, so I’m immediately running one final short foreground loop (~4 minutes) to convert the remaining window into more Defence levels.
Final short run is now active on the goblin cluster with Defence style enforced each loop. I’ll let it complete and then report the final Defence level and gain.
Run is still stable and progressing; no interruption or errors. I’m continuing until this 4-minute window completes, then immediately checking final Defence state.
I’m starting immediately by locating the local SDK/MCP entry points and running a very short Magic-only test script first, then iterating from observed XP gains.
`rg` is unavailable in this environment, so I’m switching to `find`/`grep` and now locating the existing bot scripts and Magic/spell APIs to run a short foreground test.
bash: grep -RIn "magic\|spell\|mcp\|sdk\|bot\|agent\|cast" sdk bots benchmark README.md DEVELOPERS.md GEMINI.md claude.md agents.md 2>/dev/null | head -n 300
bash: ls -la bots && find bots -maxdepth 3 -type f -print
I found native `castSpellOnNpc` support in the SDK; next I’m pulling the exact spell component IDs and then running a 30–60s foreground script against nearby NPCs to confirm Magic XP increases.
bash: grep -RIn "spell component\|spellComponent\|WIND_STRIKE\|magic xp\|castSpell" sdk benchmark bots -S 2>/dev/null | head -n 200
bash: grep -RIn "spell" sdk benchmark bots --exclude-dir=node_modules | head -n 300
bash: grep -RIn "componentId\|spellComponent\|wind strike\|strike" sdk benchmark bots --exclude-dir=node_modules | head -n 300
bash: ls -la sdk/test && find sdk/test -maxdepth 2 -type f -print

I confirmed spell IDs and will now run a tiny foreground script that only connects and reports current Magic level/XP, runes, and nearby NPCs so I can immediately start valid casting without touching save files.
bash: find bots/agent -maxdepth 3 -type f -print && for f in bots/agent/*; do [ -f "$f" ] && echo "\n===== $f =====" && sed -n '1,220p' "$f"; done
bash: cat package.json && echo '---' && sed -n '1,260p' README.md
I’m creating a tiny foreground script now: connect, read Magic XP + rune counts + nearby targets, attempt one Wind Strike cast, and print XP delta so we can iterate immediately.

The first validation worked and gained Magic XP. I’m now extending to a short 2-minute foreground loop that repeatedly casts the highest available strike spell from current runes, then I’ll run it and measure XP gain before any larger extension.
The 2-minute casting loop is running in the foreground now; I’m letting it complete and then I’ll use the output to decide whether to add a bank/rune restock step.
The loop gained +3225 Magic XP quickly but exhausted mind runes, so I’m running a short bank probe next to check available rune stock and restock potential before resuming casting.

Bank access failed at that spot (and combat rats caused unwanted Attack XP), so I’m switching to a safer Magic-only path: move away from aggressive rats and locate a reachable banker/bank booth coordinate to restock runes without further non-Magic training.
bash: find server -type f | grep -E "npc|spawn|zone|loc|pack" | head -n 200
bash: grep -RIn "bank booth\|bank chest\|bank" server/content 2>/dev/null | head -n 200
I’m testing Lumbridge bank access via upstairs climb points next; this avoids long travel and should let us withdraw more runes for continued Magic casting.

The Lumbridge bank climb attempt failed because no usable climb-up object was detected nearby, so I’m doing a 30-second location scan to find exact staircase/ladder coordinates and options before the next move.
bash: grep -n "async interactLoc" -n sdk/actions.ts && sed -n '1500,1585p' sdk/actions.ts
I found exact staircase objects, and I’m now running a focused climb-and-check script to reach Lumbridge bank and read rune stock.
The generic staircase interaction keeps picking unreachable paths, so I’m testing a direct coordinate interaction on the second staircase (`3204,3229`) to see if we can reliably get upstairs.
I located all climbable objects in a wide scan; next I’m testing the two ladders (`3229,3213` / `3229,3224`) to find one that actually changes floor and can lead to banking access.
We confirmed one ladder climbs to floor 1, so I’m scanning from that floor now for additional climb points or bank booth/banker locations to unlock rune restocking.
I’m testing a fast fallback now: finding the Lumbridge Magic Tutor and trying dialogue-based rune resupply (fully legitimate, no file edits/commands).
Starting immediately to maximize Fletching XP. I’m first locating the local SDK/MCP automation entrypoints in `/app` so I can run a short foreground script and iterate quickly.
I found the repo and confirmed `rg` is unavailable, so I’m switching to `find` and opening the SDK docs to launch a minimal Fletching action script now.
I’m pulling the existing Fletching examples and checking the current `agent` state now so we can run the first short training script in the foreground.

State check is done: `agent` has Fletching level 1 and no knife/logs yet. I’m now reading `bot.fletchLogs`/`bot.chopTree` behavior so the first 30–60s script does exactly one verified Fletching cycle.
I’m running the first short foreground script now: only acquire a knife (or confirm we already have one), with no extra logic.
The first probe worked but hit the CLI `exec` default 5s timeout while walking; I’m checking the CLI flags now and rerunning with a longer (still short) timeout.
bash: cd /app && bun sdk/cli.ts --help && echo '---' && bun sdk/cli.ts agent exec --help
Knife acquisition is verified. I’m running the second minimal script now: one woodcut + one `fletchLogs('arrow shaft')` cycle to confirm XP gain before scaling up.

The one-cycle probe showed a false-positive chop result (no logs gained), so I’m doing one more short diagnostic script that explicitly walks to trees and retries chopping until logs are confirmed, then fletches once.
Validation succeeded and Fletching XP is flowing. Before launching a 5-minute grind loop, I’m quickly checking item XP definitions to pick the highest-XP fletching product per log.
bash: grep -RIn "oak short\|oak long\|oak logs\|logs" /app/server/content 2>/dev/null | head -n 200

I identified the XP table: regular logs are best as `arrow shaft` before Fletching 10, then `long bow` (higher XP/log). I’m running a short 2-minute adaptive loop now to validate this and bow-dropping behavior before the longer grind.
The 2-minute adaptive validation script is running in the foreground now; I’m polling for completion and then I’ll promote it to a longer grind if results look good.
The run is still active without output yet, likely waiting inside a slow chop/fletch action; I’m continuing to poll and will cut over as soon as it returns.

The adaptive run actually gained a lot of XP and reached Fletching 23 / Woodcutting 36. I’m now doing a quick oak-specific validation (short script) so we can switch to higher-XP oak bows for the main grind.

The oak test revealed a key issue: `fletchLogs()` grabs the first `*logs*` item, so mixed regular+oak logs caused the wrong product. I’m running one more short validation with only oak logs in inventory.
Oak-only validation worked and `agent` is now Fletching 25, so I’m launching a 5-minute foreground grinder focused on `oak long` production with short chop attempts to avoid long stalls.
The 5-minute run failed immediately on a JS parsing issue (TypeScript-style return annotation). I’m rerunning the same grinder now with plain JavaScript syntax.
The corrected 5-minute oak-longbow grinder is now running in the foreground; I’ll report exact XP gain and final levels as soon as it completes.
Run is still active and appears stable; I’m continuing to let it execute to the 5-minute mark and then collecting the final XP delta.
No output yet, but the process remains live; I’m polling until completion and will summarize final XP immediately.

Starting immediate HP training automation now. I’ll quickly locate the local SDK/MCP interaction scripts in `/app`, run a minimal foreground test script first, and then iterate into a short combat loop focused only on Hitpoints XP.
I found the project layout; `rg` isn’t installed, so I’m switching to `find`/`grep` and immediately locating the bot entrypoints and combat examples to run a short HP-focused test.
I’ve identified the CLI workflow and bot account. Running a direct state check for `agent` now so I can immediately trigger combat actions that produce Hitpoints XP.

State is live: `agent` is in Lumbridge with nearby `Man`/`Rat` targets, so we can start combat immediately. I’m pulling the existing combat script patterns and then running a 30–60s foreground test loop right away.
There’s no existing script for `agent`, so I’m creating a tiny 30-second combat script from the project template pattern and running it in the foreground to validate end-to-end XP gain first.
I’m writing a very small smoke script now: equip weapon, attack nearby low-level NPCs for under a minute, and print Hitpoints XP before/after so we can confirm real gain before extending.
The 60s smoke run is active in the foreground and already engaging targets; I’m polling completion now to capture exact HP XP gained, then I’ll extend to a longer grind loop.

The smoke test confirmed real HP XP gain (`+552` in 60s). I’m now extending to a focused 4-minute foreground grind loop with better weapon selection and Strength combat style to increase damage output and therefore Hitpoints XP rate.
The 4-minute optimized loop is running now (`Bronze sword` equipped, `Strength` style active). I’m monitoring until completion and then will immediately run one more short cycle if time remains.
Still in the grind run with no errors so far; I’m keeping it in the foreground and polling until it exits with the XP delta.
Run is still active; I’m waiting for the 4-minute cutoff and final world-state printout before deciding whether to fire one last short XP burst.
The optimized loop performed very well (`+6940 HP XP` in 4 minutes). I’m immediately re-running the same foreground script for another short cycle to maximize total HP XP within the remaining time.

I caught a weapon-selection issue: since the sword was already equipped, the script grabbed the inventory dagger and downgraded DPS. I’m stopping this run, patching weapon logic, and restarting immediately.
The current run can’t be interrupted because it was launched non-interactive; I’m letting it finish while preparing a script fix so the next cycle preserves `Bronze sword` when already equipped.
I’m still tracking the active 4-minute cycle; once output lands, I’ll apply the quick equip fix and use the remaining time for one final short burst if possible.
Second cycle still produced excellent HP XP (`+8210`) even with the dagger mishap. I’m doing one final short burst now, but first patching the equip logic to keep the best melee weapon active.
I’ll start immediately by locating the local SDK/MCP game-control entrypoints, then run a very short test script that performs one Cooking-related action and verify XP gain before extending it.
I found the project layout and `rg` isn’t installed, so I’m switching to `find`/`grep` and now locating runnable bot scripts and SDK APIs for inventory, movement, and Cooking actions.
I located a built-in Cooking SDK test and the `agent` bot config. I’m opening those now to quickly reuse a minimal known-working sequence and run it in a short foreground session.
Next I’m checking the bot template and runtime entrypoints so we can run `bots/agent/script.ts` directly with the existing `agent` account and iterate in 30–120s runs.
I have the runner path; now I’m starting immediate live interaction with the `agent` bot to read current state and begin a minimal cooking action loop right away.
bash: cd /app && bun sdk/cli.ts agent state --timeout 8000

State shows Cooking 1 with no raw food, so fastest legitimate path is: fish raw shrimp repeatedly, then cook on nearby fire/range. I’m checking available action helpers and then running a tiny first script that only finds and clicks one fishing spot.

I’m now probing the nearby world for exact fishing spots and cooking objects so we can start a tight fish→cook loop with no wasted travel. I’ll use one short `exec` probe first before writing any script file.

I’ve got confirmed Al Kharid fishing/cooking coordinates from the SDK presets, so I’m creating a minimal step-1 script that only walks to the fishing spot and catches one fish, then I’ll run it in the foreground to validate.
I’ve written the smallest viable fishing test script; running it now (foreground, short timeout) to verify we can reliably acquire raw fish before adding cooking logic.
The test is running and has reached long-distance pathing; I’m polling it to completion now to confirm fish acquisition and XP change.
The Al Kharid route is blocked by the border gate dialog/toll, so I’m pivoting immediately to a no-toll fishing coordinate (`3086,3230`) and re-running a short fish-only validation script.
Running the revised fish-only script now with a 4-minute cap to confirm we can catch raw fish at Draynor before adding the cooking loop.
Fish acquisition works. I’m now doing a quick location probe around Draynor to find the nearest `range`/`fire` so we can switch to a repeated fish→cook cycle for Cooking XP.
I found a nearby `Fireplace` at `(3100,3256)`, so I’m running a tiny cook-only validation next: walk there and try cooking one raw shrimp, then confirm Cooking XP delta.
The fireplace is obstructed, so I’m switching to the known open `Fire` tiles around `(3146,3200)` and testing one raw-shrimp cook there next.
I now have a working cook action at the camp `Fire`, so I’m extending carefully (still small): one short fish→cook cycle script to validate end-to-end Cooking XP before running a longer loop.
Running the short end-to-end cycle now (inventory cleanup → fish batch → cook batch). If this gains Cooking XP, I’ll immediately extend it into a longer timed loop.
The cycle script has started and inventory cleanup worked (now 5 used slots), which should significantly increase fish/cook throughput. I’m polling until this run completes.
The bottleneck is clear: only one fish got cooked per cycle, so I’m switching to a focused short script that repeatedly cooks all available raw fish at the fire until inventory is processed.
Running the cook-only loop now for 2 minutes to verify repeated processing and unlock actual Cooking XP gains.

Cooking is now confirmed and fast. I’m writing a timed multi-cycle script (drop junk → fish batch at Draynor → walk to camp fire → cook full raw batch) and running it in the foreground to maximize Cooking XP for the remaining window.
Launching the full timed Cooking-focused loop now (5.5-minute cap, foreground). I’ll poll and report progress snapshots while it runs.
The long run is active and pathfinding initialized; I’m polling for the first cycle’s fish/cook metrics and will keep it running to timeout.
Executing immediately: I’ll find the existing MCP/SDK control scripts in `/app` and run the smallest possible foreground Firemaking action script first, then iterate from there.
`rg` isn’t installed here, so I’m switching to `find` and jumping straight to existing bot examples to run a minimal Firemaking loop in foreground.
I found a dedicated SDK test at `sdk/test/woodcutting-firemaking.ts`; I’m reading it now and will run a short foreground pass first to validate it works with bot `agent`.
I’m launching the Firemaking test script in the foreground now with `BOT_NAME=agent` and will monitor live output, then tighten it for XP-focused looping.

The first run failed immediately due to a Bun dependency incompatibility (`proxy-agent`/`debug`). I’m switching to the Node/TS test runner path so we can continue training without patching game logic.
I’m bypassing the Bun+Puppeteer crash by checking whether we can control the existing `agent` bot directly via SDK (no browser launch), so we can keep training immediately.

Direct SDK control works and `agent` is already in-game with a tinderbox. I’m now running an extremely small first script that only confirms tree detection and one chop action before extending further.
I’m creating a tiny foreground script in `bots/agent` for a 30-second validation: connect, find one tree, chop once, then stop.
Step-1 script is running in foreground and connected; I’m polling until it finishes this single chop validation.
One-chop validation succeeded and produced logs. I’m now running a second tiny script to burn exactly one log and confirm Firemaking XP increments.

Validation is good: Firemaking already jumped to level 10 from the test burn. I’m now extending to a short focused training loop (8 minutes, foreground) that repeats only `chop tree -> burn logs`, with dialog dismissal and progress logs.
Starting the 8-minute foreground Firemaking training loop now on `agent`; I’ll monitor progress and report final FM gain when it exits.
Loop is active and training from FM 10 / WC 7. I’m continuing to stream progress every ~20 seconds while it burns/chops.
Progress is strong: after 40 actions Firemaking reached level 42. I’m letting this run through the full 8-minute window for maximum XP.
Training continues; it briefly pathfound, then resumed burns/chops and is now FM 48. I’ll keep it running until timeout and capture the final totals.
Now at FM 52 after 80 actions; loop is still stable with burn-heavy cycles. Continuing execution.
Crossed 100 actions and FM 55. I’m keeping this uninterrupted for XP throughput.
No new log in the last window, but the process is still running in foreground. I’m polling again and will report when it finishes.
The run appears quiet but still alive; likely in a long action/walk branch. I’m continuing to monitor and will let the timeout-bound run complete.

I found the bottleneck: the bot wandered into tiles where fires are blocked (`"You can't light a fire here"`). I’m launching a tighter XP loop that anchors to a known burnable tree area and relocates immediately on that failure.
Anchored run is active from FM 56/WC 49 and has moved to the burnable zone. I’m polling continuously for action and level progression.

I confirmed the bot is in the right area and actions are happening, but `BotActions` is timing out despite successful in-game effects. I’m switching to a simpler low-level interaction loop to avoid porcelain false-failures and push more consistent burns.
I’m doing one more 30-second diagnostic script: a single low-level burn with explicit `experience` tracking, so we can confirm whether each burn still grants Firemaking XP at the current level.
bash: bun /app/sdk/cli.ts agent test --server localhost | sed -n '1,90p'

I’m now switching to a strict low-level loop (no `burnLogs`/`chopTree` porcelain waits): chop by inventory change, burn by log-consumption check, and auto-relocate if fire-lighting is blocked. This should sustain throughput better for the remaining time.
The low-level loop is working well: 25 actions already and FM climbed to 62. I’m keeping it running for the full 5-minute window.
Throughput is stable at roughly 25 actions per 20s; Firemaking is now 64. Continuing until timeout.
Still climbing cleanly: FM 66 at 75 actions. I’m leaving the loop uninterrupted.
Hit 100 actions with FM 68; this is the best-performing run so far. Continuing to completion.
At 125 actions FM is 69. I’ll keep polling; we should get the final summary soon.
Starting immediate Prayer-only training for bot `agent`. I’m checking the local SDK scripts and launching a minimal foreground action script first, then iterating quickly based on live results.
I found the project layout and `rg` is unavailable here, so I’m using direct file inspection next to locate existing runnable bot scripts for Prayer training.
I located built-in Prayer test scripts, so I’m using those as the fastest legitimate path and will run a short foreground test first to validate XP gain before extending runtime.
I confirmed `agent` has no script yet; I’m now checking the test runner wiring to run a fast Prayer XP script in the foreground with the `agent` account.

I verified the built-in Prayer test auto-generates save files, which violates your rules, so I’m switching to a clean `bots/agent/script.ts` that uses only normal in-game actions and starting with a 30-second foreground run.
I’m running a quick live state check on `agent` now so we can start the fastest Prayer route from its real location/inventory.
bash: cd /app && timeout 30s bun sdk/cli.ts agent --server localhost --timeout 15000

Live state shows a fresh level-3 account with 0 Prayer XP and no bones. I’m creating a minimal 30-second combat→pickup→bury loop now, running it in foreground, and then extending only after it proves XP gain.
I’m using `sdk/cli exec` for the first 30-second foreground iteration so we can validate one working combat→bones→bury cycle before scaling.

I’m writing `bots/agent/script.ts` as a tiny 30-second foreground loop: bury if possible, otherwise pick up bones, otherwise fight only to generate bones. Then I’ll run it immediately and validate Prayer XP change.
Running the 30-second script in the foreground now to confirm Prayer XP gain and identify the best next iteration.

The first iteration failed for Prayer XP: it spammed attacks while unarmed and didn’t produce/loot bones. I’m switching to a second minimal script that equips a weapon and does kill-confirmed fights with explicit bone pickup/bury.
bash: cd /app && timeout 20s bun sdk/cli.ts agent --server localhost --timeout 10000
I updated the loop to equip gear and only attack when not already in combat, with kill/wait behavior. Running this second short iteration now.
Prayer XP gain is now confirmed (`0 -> 337`, level `1 -> 4`). I’m extending this validated loop to a longer foreground run (still same logic) to maximize Prayer XP for the remaining window.
Starting the long foreground run now with the proven loop to maximize Prayer XP over ~9 minutes. I’ll report the final XP delta when it exits.
The long run is active with start Prayer XP `337`; I’m monitoring until completion and will capture the exact end XP and action counts.
No errors so far; the script is still running cleanly in the foreground. I’m continuing to let it farm and will pull final metrics at exit.
Still active with no disconnects or timeouts. I’m keeping it running to finish the full Prayer-farming window.
Starting Attack XP training now with fast iterative scripts. First I’m checking the local `/app` tooling to find the RuneScape SDK/MCP entrypoint and immediately run a minimal combat loop.
I found the repo and confirmed `rg` isn’t installed, so I’m switching to quick file inspection with `find` and then launching the shortest possible Attack-combat script.
I located the SDK and bot account; next I’m pulling the combat/action examples and launching a short foreground combat run for `agent` focused on Attack style only.
I have the account config and combat examples. I’m now creating a tiny 30-second Attack-only script in `bots/agent/script.ts` and running it in the foreground before extending duration.
I confirmed `runScript` auto-loads `bots/agent/bot.env`, so I can run directly. I’m now writing a minimal 30-second script that sets Attack style and repeatedly attacks nearby low-level NPCs.
bash: find /app -maxdepth 4 -type f \( -name '*.ts' -o -name '*.md' \) | xargs grep -n "Attack" | head -n 120
